← back

2023-06-05

Be Real

“Guys, quick, I’ve to do my BeReal!” I heard, in a heavy French accent right behind my back. I was on a day trip to Cadaqués, in Catalunya, and our trip guide Albert insisted that we go the “traditional” way, on a cruise ship from Roses. It was a bright Saturday morning, clear skies, and the unforgiving June sun banished me to the front corner of the ship, right in the shadow of the Captain’s cabin. The air was clean, I leaned against the railing, perhaps too precariously, and watched the wide foam trail our ship left behind on the turquoise sea, the paddle boaters sitting down for a better balance as the waves from our ship raced towards the rocky shores. Two speedboats rushed past us, keenly aware of our eyes on them, along with a group of fishermen, although I suspect they were on more of a pleasure than a work affair.



Two French girls, rushed past me to the front of the ship, also possibly disturbed by the sun, but ended up choosing a spot right in front of me. The one right next to me was in a tight black dress, spoke fluently in French to the other, and occasionally broke into songs in her language. The gust blew her auburn curly hair into my face a few times, but I didn’t complain and continued to fiddle with the camera in my hand. The views of the barren rocky shore grew monotonous, and halfway through our journey, I found myself distracted by the occasional English they spoke. They started out taking some pictures for the “gram”, posing with themselves hanging halfway outside the railing. Just as I was praying that the support doesn’t give away, a third girl ran up to them and exclaimed, “Guys, quick, I’ve to do my BeReal!”, and they posed together right behind me, and I found myself questioning if I should move or not, which would give away the gross intrusion of privacy I was engaged in, so I stayed.



BeReal. It wasn’t popular in India when it came about, and maybe it still isn’t now, but I heard about it while researching the effects on mental health from social media, especially Instagram. While Insta offered you an endless supply of self-hate fuel by methodically exposing you to lifestyle envy, BeReal promised a unique respite. For those who don’t know, the app prompts you to take a picture of your day at a random time, both with a front and back camera, to show what you’re really doing, the goal being taking away the pretense from social media. But does this really assure any kind of authenticity? Does it really provide relief from this strategic and banal self-representation we engage in every day? As a 25 years old grad student, social media is the online equivalent of hard drugs for my self-esteem so I did not dare to sign up just to give it “one try”, but I found myself contemplating throughout the trip, what it means to be ready, ready to perform an act of self-expression. “Look at the sea, this is my best BeReal ever”, she exclaimed to her friend, who I suspect nodded in agreement. Does BeReal show you the real life of your acquaintances, or is it a more calibrated way of letting your friends know you’re interesting, even when you’re unprepared, even when its not choreographed, almost lending some authenticity to the edited, planned, and timed Instagram posts that’ll inadvertently follow? The exhibition of a truly effortless, seductive, and therefore more enviable lifestyle, once a day, every day.



I took a few more pictures, as the ship banked towards Cadaqués, the small white buildings with their beige roofs almost blinding in the sun against the dark still sea. The otherwise quiet town was littered with tourists, bars, and quaint shops selling everything from Indian beach wear, to Indian hippie hemph essentials. Young children ran across the narrow uphill streets ornate with overgrown bougainvilleas, squealing and shouting in languages I’m sure only they understood. We spent the first hour guided by a French historian, Myriam, who walked us, in broken English, through the churches and folk tales surrounding Dali and Gala, and etymologies of Hebrew and Catalan words, which my boyfriend later assured were not entirely correct. I believe it was lost in the labored French-english translation Myriam wasn’t expecting to do that day.



A few hours and a perfectly cooked beef tenderloin later, I found myself standing against one of the bright white restaurant walls, facing the sea, trying to make a quick sketch of this picturesque frame which bore a stronger resemblance to Santorini than Catalunya. The bustling crowd behind me grew slower and quieter, as the day moved on, and I was pleasantly interrupted by a Cuban photographer who politely asked if I could pose for a few pictures, and I obliged. I wrapped up my sketch and called my friend who was sunbathing on the beach, and we went to get some dessert. Understandably, as it was close to 4 pm, the entire state of Catalonia was on their third nap of the day, except for one ice cream shop, with a queue of parents and sea-drenched children covered in sand waiting outside. We got our ice creams and started to look for our ride home to Barcelona, and just as I was walking past the beach, my eyes caught the two French girls in their swimsuits and a towel around their shoulders, sitting on opposite ends of a bench, deeply immersed in their phones.



The sun was still bright, I could feel the eyes of seagulls narrowing in on the cup of mango ice cream I held tightly in my hand. We turned around to take one last look at the shores of Cadaqués before we start rushing towards the bus station. The still waters, the busy group of scuba teachers preparing a group of excited tourists for their first dive, an old couple setting up a picnic blanket on the sand like they’ve done a thousand times before, it was unprepared and banal; like living the same day again and again, the new faces that found themselves on the shore everyday merging into one another; it was so boring and expected that I had forgotten to take out my phone.







write your thoughts:
